political.s: Whole Foods Market has no active PAC and OpenSecrets records show the company has not reported lobbying the federal government in recent election cycles. Any political contributions are made by individual employees, not the corporation itself. As a wholly-owned Amazon subsidiary, Whole Foods' political footprint is largely absorbed into Amazon's broader political activity.

charity.s: In 2023, Whole Foods donated 28 million meals to nearly 1,000 food rescue and redistribution programs across the country. The company's Nourishing Our Neighborhoods program has donated 50 refrigerated vans to nonprofit food rescue organizations since 2020. Through its three foundations — Whole Planet, Whole Cities, and Whole Kids — the company has committed more than $50 million to local and global communities since 2005.
charity.focus: Food security, food rescue, community nutrition, microfinance, children's health

environment.s: Whole Foods kept nearly 70% of all store and operational waste out of landfills in 2023, maintaining recycling programs at 99% of stores and composting at nearly 85% of locations. The company has nearly 64 stores with rooftop solar and 389 EV charging stations as of 2024, and was one of only three major supermarkets to receive a passing score on the 2024 Climate-Friendly Supermarkets Scorecard. Whole Foods has committed that all new stores from 2025 onward will use ultra-low GWP natural refrigerant systems, and has set a goal to cut food waste 50% by 2030.

labor.s: Workers at a Philadelphia Whole Foods voted 130–100 in January 2025 to join UFCW Local 1776, making it the first unionized Whole Foods location in company history. Whole Foods challenged the election results and NLRB charges allege the company retaliated against union supporters by firing workers and withholding region-wide wage increases. Reports have previously revealed Whole Foods used an internal 'heat map' to track stores at risk of unionization, and employees have cited chronic understaffing, stagnant wages, and productivity surveillance tied to Amazon ownership.

dei.s: Whole Foods has historically maintained DEI programs including a Diversity Champion Program, Diversity Council, and employee resource groups such as the Women's Leadership Network and Rainbow Alliance. However, as a subsidiary of Amazon — which has rolled back its DEI commitments — Whole Foods is categorized alongside companies that have not definitively sustained DEI initiatives post-2025. The company's Glassdoor DEI rating stands at 4.0 out of 5, equal to the retail sector average.

animals.s: Whole Foods has some of the most rigorous animal welfare standards in U.S. retail, requiring all fresh meat to be certified by third-party programs such as Global Animal Partnership, Certified Humane, and Certified Animal Welfare Approved. In 2024, the company expanded these standards to cover additional species including bison, veal, venison, duck, goose, and quail, with all meat department products required to carry animal welfare certification by end of 2026. Core standards prohibit antibiotics, growth hormones, crates, cages, tethers, and require welfare audits at slaughter.

guns.s: Whole Foods has a longstanding corporate no-firearms policy that prohibits customers, team members, and vendors from bringing firearms, concealed weapons, or explosives onto any store property or facility. Only commissioned law enforcement officers are exempt. The policy applies to all public areas, back rooms, parking lots, and distribution centers.

privacy.s: Whole Foods' privacy practices are governed by Amazon's Privacy Notice, meaning customer purchase data, geolocation, biometric identifiers (via Amazon One palm scanning), and browsing behavior are collected and shared within the Amazon ecosystem for targeted advertising and personalization. The company processes personal data for targeted advertising purposes and has introduced biometric palm-scan payment at checkout locations, raising significant privacy concerns among consumer advocates. Data subject rights are only supported for residents of select U.S. states, and customer email data is stored on Amazon's systems outside of Whole Foods' direct control.

execPay.s: Whole Foods is a private subsidiary of Amazon and does not file independent SEC proxy statements, making precise CEO pay ratios unavailable. CEO Jason Buechel's total compensation is not publicly disclosed as a standalone figure. Historically, pre-Amazon acquisition, Whole Foods maintained an internal pay cap of 19x the average employee salary, but this policy's status under Amazon ownership is not confirmed.

sec.note: Pay ratio extracted from proxy filing — verify manually for accuracy
